mcq.question: Fix a depth \(L \ge 1\), input dimension \(d_{\mathrm{in}}\), output dimension \(d_{\mathrm{out}} \ge 1\), and Lipschitz activation functions \(\sigma^{(1)}, \ldots, \sigma^{(L)}\). For each \(n\), let \(f_{(n)}^{(L)} : \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}} \to \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}\) be the output of a fully connected feedforward neural network with hidden-layer widths \(n_\ell = n_\ell(n)\), where \(n_0 := d_{\mathrm{in}}\), satisfying \(\lim_{n\to\infty} n_\ell / n \in (0,\infty]\) for every \(\ell = 1, \ldots, L-1\), and with i.i.d. Gaussian weights \(W_{i,j}^{(\ell)}\) such that \(\mathbb{E}[(W_{i,j}^{(\ell)})^2] = 1 / n_{\ell-1}\) for every layer \(\ell\) and every \(i,j\) (LeCun initialization). For a compact input set \(X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}\), consider the rescaled random function \(x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x)\) as an element of \(C(X, \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}})\) equipped with the topology of uniform convergence. If, for each finite subset \(X_0 \subset X\), \(\pi_{X_0}(z)\) denotes the restriction of \(z \in C(X, \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}})\) to \(X_0\), and \(I_{X_0,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}\) is the finite-dimensional rate function for the projected process on \((\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}})^{X_0}\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior, as \(n \to \infty\), of the laws of \(x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x)\) on \(C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}})\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
For every compact X ⊂ R^(d_in), the laws of the random functions x ↦ (1/√n) f_(n)^(L)(x) satisfy a large deviation principle on C(X, R^(d_out)) with speed n and rate function
I_{X,out}^(L)(z) = sup_{X_0 ⊂ X finite} I_{X_0,out}^(L)(π_{X_0}(z)).

choices.label: B
choices.text:
For every compact X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}, the laws of the random functions x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x) satisfy a large deviation principle on C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}) with speed n and rate function
\[
I_{X,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}(z)=\sup_{\substack{X_0\subset X\ \mathrm{finite}\\ |X_0|\le 2}} I_{X_0,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}\bigl(\pi_{X_0}(z)\bigr).
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text: For every compact X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}, the laws of the random functions x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x) are exponentially tight on C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}) with speed n.

choices.label: D
choices.text:
For every compact X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}, once the projected processes \(\pi_{X_0}((1/\sqrt{n})f_{(n)}^{(L)})\) satisfy LDPs for all finite \(X_0\subset X\), it follows automatically that the laws of x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x) satisfy a full large deviation principle on C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}) with speed n and rate function
\[
I_{X,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}(z)=\sup_{X_0\subset X\ \mathrm{finite}} I_{X_0,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}\bigl(\pi_{X_0}(z)\bigr),
\]
without any separate exponential tightness argument.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
For every compact X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}, the laws of the random functions x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x) satisfy a large deviation principle on C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}) with speed n and rate function
\[
I_{X,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}(z)=\max_{X_0\subset X\ \mathrm{finite}} I_{X_0,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}\bigl(\pi_{X_0}(z)\bigr).
\]

theorem:
[Erd\H{o}s]\label{thm:dens}
    For a dissociated set $\mathcal S$, we have $\left\vert\mathcal S(n)\right\vert \le \log_2 n + \mathcal{O}( \log_2\log_2 n)$.
sketch:
Consider the map
$$f: \mathcal P\left(\mathcal S(n)\right) \to \mathbb N$$
that takes a subset of $\mathcal S(n)$ to the sum of its elements. Since $\mathcal S$ is dissociated, this map is injective. So,
$$2^{\left\vert\mathcal S(n)\right\vert} \leq n\cdot \left\vert\mathcal S(n)\right\vert +1,$$
which yields the claimed bound $\left\vert\mathcal S(n)\right\vert \le \log_2 n + \mathcal O(\log_2\log_2 n)$.

mcq.question: Let \(\mathcal S\) be a dissociated set of positive integers, meaning that all finite subset sums of distinct elements of \(\mathcal S\) are different (equivalently, if \(\sum_{s\in F}\varepsilon_s s=0\) for some finite \(F\subseteq \mathcal S\) with coefficients \(\varepsilon_s\in\{-1,0,1\}\), then all \(\varepsilon_s=0\)). For \(n\ge 1\), define \(\mathcal S(n)=\mathcal S\cap\{1,2,\dots,n\}\), and let \(\log_2\) denote the base-2 logarithm. As \(n\to\infty\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic upper bound for \(|\mathcal S(n)|\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \[|\mathcal S(n)|\le \log_2 n+\mathcal O\!\bigl(\log_2\log_2 n\bigr).\]

choices.label: B
choices.text: \[|\mathcal S(n)|\le \log_2 n+\mathcal O(1).\]

choices.label: C
choices.text: \[|\mathcal S(n)|\le \log_2 n+o(\log_2 n).\]

choices.label: D
choices.text: \[|\mathcal S(n)|\le \log_2 n+o\!\bigl(\log_2\log_2 n\bigr).\]

choices.label: E
choices.text: \[|\mathcal S(n)|\le \log_2 n+\tfrac13\,\log_2\log_2 n+\mathcal O(1).\]

sketch: To prove the main theorem, the approach is to reduce the integro-differential equation to a pair of integral equations for the derivative of its solution, coupled at a point $u_0\in(0,\infty)$. On $[u_0,\infty)$, one treats a somewhat exotic terminal value problem, seeking a solution with asymptotic behavior $u^{-\gamma}$ as $u\to\infty$; for sufficiently large $u_0$ this solution is obtained using the Banach fixed point theorem, and uniqueness is established. On $(0,u_0]$, the derivative satisfies a Volterra integral equation of the second kind with a terminal condition at $u_0$ coming from the first part. Gluing the solutions together gives the derivative (with a multiplicative constant $C$ to determine), then integrating yields $\Phi$ with an additive constant $C_0$, which must be taken as zero to enforce $\Phi(0)=0$. The constant $C$ is chosen so that the limit at infinity equals one, and $C$ is calculable via an integral involving the constructed solutions. Finally, using It\^o's formula, one concludes that $\Phi$ coincides with the survival probability. The method avoid[s] reducing the IDE to a higher-order ODE and instead uses standard results concerning the uniqueness of solutions to IDEs.

mcq.question:
Consider the annuity risk model in which the capital reserve process \(X^u=(X_t^u)\) with initial capital \(u\ge 0\) satisfies
\[
X_t^u=u+\int_0^t \kappa X_s^u(a\,ds+\sigma\,dW_s)+\int_0^t r(1-\kappa)X_s^u\,ds-ct+\sum_{i=1}^{N_t}\xi_i,
\]
where \(W\) is a standard Wiener process, \(N\) is a Poisson process with intensity \(\lambda>0\), independent of \(W\), and \((\xi_i)_{i\ge1}\) are i.i.d. positive random variables, independent of \(N\), with distribution function \(F\) on \([0,\infty)\). Let
\[
\tau^u:=\inf\{t\ge 0:X_t^u\le 0\},\qquad \Phi(u):={\bf P}(\tau^u=\infty),\qquad \Psi(u):=1-\Phi(u).
\]
Assume \(F(0)=0\), \({\bf E}[\xi]<\infty\), \(\sigma>0\), and
\[
\gamma:=\frac{(a-r)\kappa+r}{\kappa^2\sigma^2}>1.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior of the ruin probability \(\Psi(u)\) as \(u\to\infty\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)\sim C\,u^{-\gamma+1}\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

choices.label: B
choices.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)\sim C\,u^{-\gamma}\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

choices.label: C
choices.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)=O\!\left(u^{-\gamma+1}\right)\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

choices.label: D
choices.text:
There exists \(\varepsilon>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)=O\!\left(u^{-\gamma+1-\varepsilon}\right)\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

choices.label: E
choices.text:
There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Phi(u)=1-C\,u^{-\gamma}+o\!\left(u^{-\gamma}\right)
\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

theorem:
\label{main theorem} (\begin{thm}\label{thm full Fourier expansion}
Suppose $\ell> n$, $D\equiv 3\modulo 4$ and $n\equiv 2\modulo 4$. 
The constant term along $\bff{Z}$ of the Eisenstein series   
$E_\ell(g, s=\ell+1)$ has the Fourier expansion 
\begin{align*}
   E_{\ell,0}(g)+\sum_{\substack{0\neq T\in\bff{V}_{0}\\\lrangle{T}{T}\geq 0}}a_{T}(g_{f})\call{W}_{T}(g_{\infty}),\,g=g_{f}g_{\infty}\in\bff{G}(\A_{f})\times \bff{G}(\R),
\end{align*}
where the Fourier coefficients satisfy:
\begin{itemize}
    \item The locally constant functions $a_{T}(g_{f})$ are valued in $\Q$ when restricted to $g_{f}\in\bff{M}(\A_{f})$.
    \item The $\bff{N}$-constant term $E_{\ell,0}(g)$ is a rational multiple of $|\nu(g)|_{\A_{E}}^{\ell+1}\frac{\zeta_{E}(\ell+1)}{\pi^{2\ell+1}}[u_{1}^{n}][u_{2}^{n}]$ when restricted to $g\in \bff{M}(\A)$.
\end{itemize}
Moreover, when $g_{f}=1$, 
$E_{\ell}(g_{\infty},s=\ell+1)$ have the following explicit Fourier expansion:
\begin{align*}
   E_{\ell,0}(g_\infty)+C_{\ell}\sum_{0\neq T\in\bff{V}_{0}(\Z)\atop \lrangle{T}{T}=0}\sigma_{E,\ell}(T)\call{W}_{T}(g_{\infty})
    +D_{n,\ell}\sum_{T\in\bff{V}_{0}(\Z)\atop \lrangle{T}{T}>0}\left(\prod_{p|\lrangle{T}{T}}Q_{T,p}(p^{\ell-\frac{n-1}{2}})\right)\call{W}_{T}(g_{\infty}),
\end{align*}
where
\[C_{\ell}=\frac{(-1)^{\ell}2^{2\ell+1}}{(\ell!)^{2}}\quad \text{and} \quad D_{n,\ell}=\frac{2^{2n+2}(2\ell-n+2)D^{\ell+1-\frac{n}{2}}}{(\ell!)^{2}\left|B_{2\ell-n+2}\right|\sigma_{\ell+1-\frac{n}{2}}(D)} \in \Bbb Q.\]
\end{thm})
Assume that the discriminant of $E$ is odd.
When $\ell>n$, the Eisenstein series   
$E_\ell(g)$
has rational Fourier coefficients in the following sense:
\begin{itemize}
    \item The locally constant functions $a_{T}(E_{\ell},g_{f})$ take values in $\mathbb{Q}$ when restricted to $\mathbf{M}(\A_{f})$.
    \item The constant term $E_{\ell,\mathbf{N}}(g)$ is a rational multiple of $|\nu(g)|_{\A_{E}}^{\ell+1}\cdot\frac{\zeta_{E}(\ell+1)}{\pi^{2\ell+1}}[u_{1}^{n}][u_{2}^{n}]$ when restricted to $\mathbf{M}(\mathbb{A})$, where $[u_{1}^{n}][u_{2}^{n}]$ denotes a vector in a fixed basis of $\mathbb{V}_{\ell}$. 
\end{itemize}
Moreover, the value of the $T$th Fourier coefficient $a_{T}(E_{\ell},g_{f})$ at $g_{f}=1$ 
is non-zero if and only if $T$ lies in a self-dual Hermitian $\oo_{E}$-lattice $\mathbf{V}_{0}(\mathbb{Z})$ of $\mathbf{V}_{0}$.
We have an explicit formula for the Fourier coefficients $a_{T}(E_{\ell},1)$,
and their denominators are all divisors of an integer depending only on $E,\ell$, and $n$.,
sketch: The Fourier coefficients can be written as certain Eulerian integrals, so in establishing the main theorem, the proof is reduced to local computations at $g_{f}=1$. The most complicated part is computing $a_{T}(E_{\ell},1)$ for anisotropic $T\in\mathbf{V}_{0}$, and the strategy is to consider $\mathbf{V}_{0}$ as a quadratic space, with symmetric bilinear form $\tr_{E/\mathbb{Q}}\lrangle{\,}{\,}$. \begin{itemize} \item (Finite places) For a prime $p$, the $\Q_{p}$-component of $a_{T}(E_{\ell},1)$ is a combination of several quadratic Siegel series. If $p$ is unramified in $E$, they directly apply a result from prior work on the Siegel series, while if $p$ is ramified, they prove a ramified version of this result. In both cases the local factor is the product of $(1-p^{n-2\ell-2})$ with $p^{(n-2\ell-1)v_{p}(\lrangle{T}{T})}Q_{T,p}(p^{\ell-\frac{n-1}{2}})$ (up to some rational factor independent of $T$ when $p$ is ramified), where $Q_{T,p}$ lies in $\mathbb{Z}[X^{2}]+p^{1/2}X\mathbb{Z}[X^{2}]$ and satisfies a certain functional equation. Hence the product of finite components is a rational multiple of $\zeta(2\ell-n+2)^{-1}\lrangle{T}{T}^{n-2\ell-1}$, with denominator bounded by an integer independent of $T$. \item (Archimedean place) The archimedean component is the Fourier transform (on $\mathbf{V}_{0}(\mathbb{R})$, viewed as a quadratic space) of a complicated function $F_{0,\ell}$. Following the strategy of prior work, they compare $F_{0,\ell}$ to the inverse Fourier transform of $\mathrm{Char}(\lrangle{T}{T}>0)\lrangle{T}{T}^{2\ell-n+1}\mathcal{W}_{T}(1)$, and obtain that the archimedean contribution is the product of $\pi^{2\ell-n+2}\lrangle{T}{T}^{2\ell-n+1}$ with a rational number independent of $T$. \end{itemize} Combining the two parts together, they conclude that for anisotropic $T$, the coefficient $a_{T}(E_{\ell},1)$ is a rational number with a uniformly bounded denominator, yielding the first rationality (or algebraicity) result for a family of quaternionic Eisenstein series.

mcq.question: Assume \(\ell>n\), \(D\equiv 3\pmod 4\), and \(n\equiv 2\pmod 4\). Let \(\mathbf P=\mathbf M\mathbf N\) be the parabolic subgroup stabilizing a fixed isotropic line, with Levi factor \(\mathbf M\simeq \mathrm{Res}_{E/\mathbb Q}(\mathbf G_m)\times \mathbf U(\mathbf V_0)\), where \(\mathbf V_0\subset \mathbf V\) has signature \((1,n-1)\). Write \(\mathbf Z=[\mathbf N,\mathbf N]\), so that \(\mathbf N(\mathbb Q)/\mathbf Z(\mathbb Q)\) is identified with \(\mathbf V_0\). Let \(E_\ell(g,s)\) be the Eisenstein series under consideration, evaluated at \(s=\ell+1\), and write the adelic variable as \(g=g_f g_\infty\in \mathbf G(\mathbb A_f)\times \mathbf G(\mathbb R)\). Let \(E_{\ell,0}(g)\) denote the \(\mathbf N\)-constant term appearing in its \(\mathbf Z\)-Fourier expansion, let \(\mathcal W_T(g_\infty)\) denote the archimedean \(T\)-th Fourier--Whittaker term, and let \(\nu\) be the similitude character. Which of the following conclusions about the Fourier expansion at \(s=\ell+1\) is valid under these hypotheses?

correct_choice.text:

\\[

\\]
Moreover:
1. For \\(g_f\\in \\mathbf M(\\mathbb A_f)\\), each locally constant coefficient \\(a_T(g_f)\\) takes values in \\(\\mathbb Q\\).
2. For \\(g\\in \\mathbf M(\\mathbb A)\\), the term \\(E_{\\ell,0}(g)\\) is a rational multiple of
\\[
|\\nu(g)|_{\\mathbb A_E}^{\\ell+1}\\frac{\\zeta_E(\\ell+1)}{\\pi^{2\\ell+1}}[u_1^n][u_2^n].
\\]

\\[

+C_\\ell\\sum_{\\substack{0\\neq T\\in \\mathbf V_0(\\mathbb Z)\\\\ \\langle T,T\\rangle=0}} \\sigma_{E,\\ell}(T)\\,\\mathcal W_T(g_\\infty)

sketch: Building on Kable prior work, the authors first determine explicitly the linear maps describing the Lie algebra action between the multiplicity spaces occurring in the (not multiplicity-free) restriction of $\pi_{\varepsilon,s}$ to $K$. \begin{proposition} \label{Mmatrix} The proof has $$(\RC_{l}\boxtimes \RC_{l'})R_s^{\pm}(n,m)\zeta^{n,m}_{a}=M^{\pm}_{l,l'}\zeta^{n+3-2l,m+1-2l'}_{a\pm 1}, \qquad (0\le l\le 3, 0\le l'\le 1), $$ where the matrices $M^\pm=(M^\pm_{l,l'})_{0\leq l\leq 3,0\leq l'\leq1}$ are given by $$M^+:=\left(\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1}{2} (2s -2 a+m+n) & \frac{(a-m) (2s - 2 a - m-2 + n)}{4 m} \\ \frac{(3 a-n) (6s - 6a -6 + 3 m + n)}{12 n} & \frac{(a-m) (3 a-n) (6 s - 6 a - 12 +n-3 m)}{24 m n} \\ \frac{(3 a-n) (n - 3a - 2) (6 a- 6 s - 3 m+n+8)}{24 (n-1) n} & \frac{(a-m) (n - 3 a) (n - 3 a-2) (6s - 6 a - 14 - 3 m - n)}{48 m (n-1) n} \\ \frac{(n - 3a) (n - 3 a - 2) (n - 3 a - 4) (2 a - 2s -m+n+2)}{16 (n-2) (n-1) n} & \frac{(a-m) (3 a-n) (n - 3 a - 2) (n - 3 a - 4) (2s - 2 a - 4 - m - n)}{32 m (n-2) (n-1) n} \\ \end{array} \right) $$ and $$M^-:= \left(\begin{array}{cc} - \frac{1}{2} (2s + 2 a+m+n) & \frac{(-a-m) (2s + 2 a - m-2 + n)}{4 m} \\ \frac{(- 3 a-n) (6s + 6a -6 + 3 m + n)}{12 n} & \frac{(a+m) (-3 a-n) (6 s + 6 a - 12 +n-3 m)}{24 m n} \\ \frac{(3 a+n) (n + 3a - 2) (-6 a- 6 s - 3 m+n+8)}{24 (n-1) n} & \frac{(-a-m) (n + 3 a) (n + 3 a-2) (6s + 6 a - 14 - 3 m - n)}{48 m (n-1) n} \\ \frac{(n + 3a) (n + 3 a - 2) (n + 3 a - 4) (- 2 a - 2s -m+n+2)}{16 (n-2) (n-1) n} & \frac{(a+m) (- 3 a-n) (n + 3 a - 2) (n + 3 a - 4) (2s + 2 a - 4 - m - n)}{32 m (n-2) (n-1) n} \\ \end{array} \right).$$ \end{proposition} They then identify a convenient basis of every multiplicity space that is adapted to the transition between $K$-types. With respect to this basis, the action of the Knapp--Stein intertwining operators on each $K$-isotypic component is upper triangular, and they determine the corresponding eigenvalues. \begin{corollary}\label{cor:ClosedFormulasIntertwinerMultOneKtypes} For $r\in\ZZ_{\geq0}$ and $s\in\CC$, $\tilde{s}=3-s$: \begin{align*} A_{4r,0} &= \frac{(\frac{\tilde{s}}{2})_r(\frac{3\tilde{s}-3}{2})_r}{(\frac{s}{2})_r(\frac{3s-3}{2})_r} A_{0,0}, & A_{4r+2,0} &= \frac{(\frac{\tilde{s}+1}{2})_r(\frac{3\tilde{s}-2}{2})_r}{(\frac{s+1}{2})_r(\frac{3s-2}{2})_r} A_{2,0},\\ A_{0,4r} &= \frac{(\frac{\tilde{s}}{2})_r^2(\frac{3\tilde{s}-1}{6})_{r}(\frac{3\tilde{s}+1}{6})_{r}}{(\frac{s}{2})_r^2(\frac{3s-1}{6})_{r}(\frac{3s+1}{6})_{r}} A_{0,0}, & A_{0,4r+2} &= \frac{(\frac{\tilde{s}+1}{2})_r(\frac{\tilde{s}-1}{2})_{r+1}(\frac{3\tilde{s}+2}{6})_{r}(\frac{3\tilde{s}-2}{6})_{r+1}}{ (\frac{s+1}{2})_r(\frac{s-1}{2})_{r+1}(\frac{3s+2}{6})_{r}(\frac{3s-2}{6})_{r+1} } A_{2,0},\\ A_{2,4r+2} &= \frac{(\frac{\tilde{s}}{2})_{r+1}(\frac{\tilde{s}}{2})_r(\frac{3\tilde{s}+1}{6})_r(\frac{3\tilde{s}-1}{6})_{r+1}}{(\frac{s}{2})_{r+1}(\frac{s}{2})_r(\frac{3s+1}{6})_r(\frac{3s-1}{6})_{r+1}} A_{0,0}, & A_{2,4r} &= multiplicity-one $K$-types as follows, where $m,r\in\ZZ_{\geq0}$: \begin{enumerate} \item The eigenvalues of $A_{3m+2r,m}(s)$ are given by \begin{align*} \mu_{2j}(3m+2r,m,s) &= \end{align*} \item The eigenvalues of $A_{3m,m+2r}(s)$ are given by \begin{align*} \mu_{2j}(3m,m+2r,s) &= \frac{(\frac{\tilde{s}-2j-r-2}{2})_{2j+1}(\frac{r+\tilde{s}+1}{2}+j)_j(\frac{r-\tilde{s}+3}{2}+j)_j(r+\tilde{s}+4j+1)_{m-2j-1}}{(\frac{s-2j-r-2}{2})_{2j+1}(\frac{r+s+1}{2}+j)_j(\frac{r-s+3}{2}+j)_j(r+s+4j+1)_{m-2j-1}} A_{0,2(r+2j+1)}(s). \end{align*} \item The eigenvalues of $A_{3m+2,m+2r}(s)$ are given by \begin{align*} \mu_{2j}(3m+2,m+2r,s) &= \frac{(\frac{\tilde{s}-r-2j}{2})_{2j}(\frac{r+\tilde{s}+1}{2}+j)_j(\frac{r-\tilde{s}+3}{2}+j)_j(r+\tilde{s}+4j+1)_{m-2j}}{(\frac{s-r-2j}{2})_{2j}(\frac{r+s+1}{2}+j)_j(\frac{r-s+3}{2}+j)_j(r+s+4j+1)_{m-2j}}A_{2,2(r+2j)}(s),\\ \end{align*} \item The eigenvalues of $A_{3m+4,m+2r}(s)$ are given by \begin{align*} \mu_{2j}(3m+4,m+2r,s) &= \frac{(\frac{\tilde{s}-r-2j+1}{2})_{2j}(\frac{r+\tilde{s}}{2}+j+1)_j(\frac{r-\tilde{s}}{2}+j+2)_j(r+\tilde{s}+4j+2)_{m-2j}}{(\frac{s-r-2j+1}{2})_{2j}(\frac{r+s}{2}+j+1)_j(\frac{r-s}{2}+j+2)_j(r+s+4j+2)_{m-2j}}A_{4,2(r+2j)}(s),\\ mcq.question:
Let \(G=G_{2(2)}\) be the split simple real Lie group, and let \(P=MAN\) be the (up to conjugation unique) parabolic subgroup whose unipotent radical \(N\) is a \(5\)-dimensional Heisenberg group. Write \(\operatorname{sgn}:M\to\{\pm1\}\) for the nontrivial character of \(M/M_0\), and let \(\chi_s\) \((s\in\mathbb C)\) denote the standard characters of \(A\). For \(\varepsilon\in\{0,1\}\), define the degenerate principal series representation
\[
\pi_{\varepsilon,s}=\operatorname{Ind}_P^G(\operatorname{sgn}^\varepsilon\otimes \chi_s\otimes 1).
\]
Which statement is equivalent to the reducibility of \(\pi_{\varepsilon,s}\)? Here \(\mathbb Z\) denotes the integers and \(\mathbb Z_{\ge 0}\) the nonnegative integers.

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(\pi_{\varepsilon,s}\) is reducible exactly in the following cases: either \(\varepsilon=0\) and \(s\in \mathbb Z\cup\left(2+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\cup\left(1-\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\), or \(\varepsilon=1\) and \(s\in \mathbb Z\cup\left(\frac{7}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\cup\left(\frac{2}{3}-\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(\pi_{\varepsilon,s}\) is reducible exactly in the following cases: either \(\varepsilon=0\) and \(s\in \mathbb Z_{\ge 0}\cup\left(2+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\cup\left(1-\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\), or \(\varepsilon=1\) and \(s\in \mathbb Z_{\ge 0}\cup\left(\frac{7}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\cup\left(\frac{2}{3}-\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: If \(\pi_{\varepsilon,s}\) is reducible, then either \(\varepsilon=0\) and \(s\in \mathbb Z\cup\left(2+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\cup\left(1-\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\), or \(\varepsilon=1\) and \(s\in \mathbb Z\cup\left(\frac{7}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\cup\left(\frac{2}{3}-\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(\pi_{\varepsilon,s}\) is reducible exactly in the following cases: either \(\varepsilon=0\) and \(s\in \mathbb Z\cup\left(2+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\cup\left(1-\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\), or \(\varepsilon=1\) and \(s\in \mathbb Z\cup\left(\frac{7}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\cup\left(1-\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\).

choices.text: \(\pi_{\varepsilon,s}\) is reducible exactly when either \(\varepsilon=0\) and \(s\) or \(3-s\) belongs to \(\mathbb Z_{\ge3}\cup\left(2+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\), or \(\varepsilon=1\) and \(s\) or \(3-s\) belongs to \(\mathbb Z_{\ge3}\cup\left(\frac{7}{3}+\frac{2}{3}\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\right)\).

mcq.question: Let \(\mathbf V\) be a pseudovariety of semigroups (that is, a class of finite semigroups closed under finite direct products, subsemigroups, and homomorphic images), and assume that every group in \(\mathbf V\) is solvable. Here \(N\) denotes the size of the ambient finite semigroup, and a straight-line program is a sequence that builds elements from the given generators using semigroup multiplication, with length equal to the number of multiplication steps. Which statement is equivalent to the assertion that \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of length \(\mathcal O((\log N)^2)\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of length \(\mathcal O(\log N)\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of width \(\mathcal O(1)\) and length \(\mathcal O(\log N)\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) affords efficient compression, i.e. admits straight-line programs of length \(\operatorname{polylog} N\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) admits straight-line programs of width \(2\) and length \(\mathcal O(\operatorname{polylog} N)\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: \(\mathbf V\) contains neither \(\mathbf{LRB}\) nor \(\mathbf{RRB}\), and contains no nonsolvable group.

theorem:
\label{MainTheorem}
 Let ${\mathbf K}$ be ${\mathbf N}$-$2$-AD regular. Suppose that the coefficients of the operator ${\mathcal L}$ belong to $C^3$. Then   function 
 $f$ defined on ${\mathbf K}$ 
 belongs to the class $\Hc_{{\mathcal L}}^{{\mathbf r}+\omega}({\mathbf K})$ if 
and only if
for any $\delta< \frac14\operatorname{diam\,}({\mathbf K})$, there exists an approximating 
function
 $v_\delta(x), \, x\in\Kb_\delta,$ such that, with some constant
$\mathbbm{c}>0$,
\begin{gather}\label{appr.function}
  \Lc_y v_\delta(y)=0, \, y\in \Kb_{\delta};\\\nonumber
  |v_\delta(x)-f(x)|\le \mathbbm{c} \delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta), \, x\in 
  {\mathbf K};\\\nonumber
  |v_\delta(y)-v_{\delta/2}(y)|\le \mathbbm{c}\delta^{\mathbf r}\omega(\delta), \, 
  y\in\Kb_{\frac{\delta}{2}}.
\end{gather}
sketch: The introduction explains the structure of the proof of the main theorem as follows. 1. Start with $f\in \Hc_{{\mathcal L}}^{{\mathbf r}+\omega}({\mathbf K})$ and construct a special extension $f_0$ to a fixed neighborhood $\Omega$ of ${\mathbf K}$. 2. For $f_0$, use the Green function $G^{\circ}(x,y)$ of ${\mathcal L}$ in $\Omega$ to establish the integral representation \begin{equation}\label{intro.repres} f_0(x)=\int_{\Om}\Lc f_0(y)G^{\circ}(x,y)dy, \, x\in \Om. \end{equation} Because $f_0$ may have derivatives that behave badly when approaching ${\mathbf K}$, the representation requires detailed control of ${\mathcal L}f_0(x)$ and derivatives of $f_0(x)$ near ${\mathbf K}$. 3. Obtain this control via complicated estimates for the Green function $G_{x,r}(x,y)$ in balls $B_r(x)$ and its derivatives up to the third order in $x,y$, and also in the parameter variable $\varsigma$. Some estimates are derived using Schauder-type approach, and others are taken from Ju.~Krasovskii prior work and M.~Gr\"uter--K.-O.~Widman prior work. 4. Define the approximants by \[ v_{\delta}(x)=\int_{\Omega\setminus \Kb_{\delta}}{\mathcal L} f_0(y)G^{\circ}(x,y)\,dy, \] with addition of a collection of several compensatory ${\mathcal L}$-harmonic terms of a simpler nature, namely (as indicated by the reference) \begin{equation}\label{35} v_{\de}(x)=c_\Nb\int\limits_{\Om\setminus \Kb'_\de}G(x,y)\Lc f_0(y)dy+\sum_{\n=1}^{N} F_\n(x). \end{equation} Then the fact that $v_{\delta}$ is ${\mathcal L}$-harmonic in $\Kb_{\delta}$ is obvious from the definition of $G^{\circ}(x,y)$. 5. The estimates giving the approximation quality (the bounds in \begin{gather}\label{appr.function} \Lc_y v_\de(y)=0, \, y\in \Kb_{\de};\\\nonumber |v_\de(x)-f(x)|\le \mathbbm{c} \de^\rb\om(\de), \, x\in \Kb;\\\nonumber |v_\de(y)-v_{\de/2}(y)|\le \mathbbm{c}\de^\rb\om(\de), \, y\in\Kb_{\frac{\de}{2}}. \end{gather}) follow from the estimates for the function $f_0(x)$ and its derivatives. The paper outline reiterates that next it constructs $f_0$, proves the integral representation, and thereby present[s] the required approximation… thus proving the main theorem.

mcq.question: Let \(\mathbf K\subset \mathbb R^{\mathbf N}\) be a compact \((\mathbf N-2)\)-Ahlfors-David regular set, let \(\omega\) be a continuity modulus, and let \(\mathcal L=-\nabla\!\cdot(\mathbf a(x)\nabla)\) be a formally self-adjoint second-order elliptic operator with \(C^3\) coefficients defined on a neighborhood of \(\mathbf K\). Write \(\mathbf K_\rho\) for the \(\rho\)-neighborhood of \(\mathbf K\). Which statement is equivalent to the condition that a function \(f\) on \(\mathbf K\) belongs to the class \(\mathcal H_{\mathcal L}^{\mathbf r+\omega}(\mathbf K)\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B

theorem:
\label{thm : thm1 intro}
      For each positive (or negative simple) root $\beta$, there exists a unique (up to isomorphism) strongly $\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}$-exact complex $C_{\bullet}[\beta]$ in $\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}$ such that $C_n[\beta]=0$ if $n<0$ and $C_0[\beta]$ is indecomposable and isomorphic to $Y[\beta]$.

mcq.question: Let \(Q\) be a quiver, let \(\mathcal H_Q^{(1)}\) be the associated additive monoidal category, and let \(\mathcal K_Q^{(1)}\) denote its bounded homotopy category. Fix a root \(\beta\) that is either positive or a negative simple root, and let \(Y[\beta]\) be the associated indecomposable object of \(\mathcal H_Q^{(1)}\). In \(\mathcal K_Q^{(1)}\), which object is uniquely determined up to isomorphism by \(\beta\) among the strongly \(\mathcal H_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complexes?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A unique (up to isomorphism) strongly \(\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complex \(C_{\bullet}[\beta]\in\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}\) such that \(C_n[\beta]=0\) for all \(n<1\) and \(C_1[\beta]\) is indecomposable with \(C_1[\beta]\cong Y[\beta]\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: A strongly \(\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complex \(C_{\bullet}[\beta]\in\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}\) such that \(C_n[\beta]=0\) for all \(n<0\) and \(C_0[\beta]\) is indecomposable with \(C_0[\beta]\cong Y[\beta]\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: A unique (up to isomorphism) \(\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complex \(C_{\bullet}[\beta]\in\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}\) such that \(C_n[\beta]=0\) for all \(n<0\) and \(C_0[\beta]\) is indecomposable with \(C_0[\beta]\cong Y[\beta]\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: A unique (up to isomorphism) strongly \(\mathcal{H}_Q^{(1)}\)-exact complex \(C_{\bullet}[\beta]\in\mathcal{K}_Q^{(1)}\) such that \(C_n[\beta]=0\) for all \(n<0\), \(C_0[\beta]\) is indecomposable with \(C_0[\beta]\cong Y[\beta]\), and each differential \(d_n\) admits a weak cokernel in \(\mathcal H_Q^{(1)}\).

mcq.question: Let \(M\cong \mathbb Z^n\) be a lattice, \(T=\operatorname{Spec}\mathbb C[M]\cong (\mathbb C^*)^n\), and let \(f=\sum_{m\in \Delta\cap M} a_m x^m\) be a nondegenerate Laurent polynomial with \(n\)-dimensional Newton polytope \(\Delta\). Write \(L(\Delta):=\bigoplus_{m\in \Delta\cap M}\mathbb C\,x^m\), and let \(\Int(\cdot)\) denote interior (relative interior on faces such as facets \(\Gamma\)). Assume that \(\Int(\Delta)\cap M\) is not contained in any hyperplane. In the notation where \(d\mathcal P_{B,f}^k\) is the differential under consideration and \(g_{\Gamma}(f)\) is the element associated to a facet \(\Gamma\le \Delta\), which subspace gives \(\ker(d\mathcal P_{B,f}^k)\) modulo \(\Lie(T)\)?

theorem:
\label{prop1.1}
   A minimally intersecting filling pair $(\alpha, \beta)$ of a closed surface $S_2$ of genus two is of either type $\{4, 12\}$ or type $\{8,8\}$ (equivalently, there does not exist a filling pair of type $\{6,10\}$ on $S_2$).
sketch: The proof of the stated theorem relies on the theory of fat graphs. Let $\Sigma$ be the set of all minimally intersecting filling pairs on $S_2$, with an action of $\mathrm{Mod}(S_2)$ given by $f\cdot(\alpha,\beta)=(f(\alpha),f(\beta))$. In the course of establishing the main theorem, one proves the following theorem. \begin{theorem}\label{thm1.2} There are exactly two (up to mapping class group action) minimal filling pairs in $S_2$. \end{theorem} Its proof is based on fat graph theory and follows the approach of Aougab and Huang (as in Lemma 2.3 of prior work). This classification gives an advantage for minimizing lengths: one treats the length problem in two cases, the $\{4,12\}$ case and the $\{8,8\}$ case (the theorems below). \begin{theorem}\label{thm1.3} Let $(\alpha, \beta)$ be a $\{8,8\}$ filling pair in a hyperbolic surface $X\in \mathcal{T}_2$. Then $$\mathrm{length}_X(\alpha,\beta) \geq 8\cdot\cosh^{-1}\left(\sqrt{2}+1\right)\approx 12.228567.$$ \end{theorem} The proof of the theorem above is based on studying the angles at the intersection points of the geodesic representatives of the curves and applying the isoperimetric inequality for hyperbolic polygons. \begin{theorem}\label{thm1.4} Let $(\alpha, \beta)$ be a $\{4,12\}$ filling pair in a hyperbolic surface $X\in \mathcal{T}_2$. Then $$\mathrm{length}_X(\alpha,\beta) \geq L_0,$$ where $L_0=6 \cdot \cosh^{-1}(7/2)\approx 11.5490838.$ \end{theorem} The proof of the theorem above builds on these ideas and also uses numerical computations; finally, the lower bound from the preceding theorem is shown to give a global lower bound for any filling pair on a genus-two surface.

mcq.question: Let \((\alpha,\beta)\) be a minimally intersecting filling pair on a closed orientable surface \(S_2\) of genus 2; that is, \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are homotopically distinct simple closed curves in minimal position, their complement \(S_2\setminus(\alpha\cup\beta)\) is a disjoint union of topological disks, and their geometric intersection number is minimal among filling pairs on \(S_2\). For such a pair on \(S_2\), the complement consists of two polygonal disk regions. If the type \(\{m,n\}\) records the unordered pair of side counts of these complementary regions, which types can occur?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: Exactly the types \(\{4,12\}\) and \(\{8,8\}\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The only combinatorial restrictions are that \(m\) and \(n\) are even and that \(m+n=16\); hence the possible types are exactly \(\{4,12\}\), \(\{6,10\}\), and \(\{8,8\}\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The intermediate type \(\{6,10\}\) does not occur for a minimally intersecting filling pair on \(S_2\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exactly the types \(\{4,12\}\) and \(\{6,10\}\) can occur; the balanced type \(\{8,8\}\) cannot arise because the two complementary disks cannot be combinatorially identical.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Exactly the types \(\{6,10\}\) and \(\{8,8\}\) can occur; a complementary \(4\)-gon is impossible for curves already in pairwise minimal position.

sketch:
To prove the main theorem, the paper builds upon the approach of Gao et al. by finding a collection of $k$ admissible paths between any two given vertices $x,y$ in a suitable 2-connected graph $G$. The key step is to identify a specified subgraph $H$ (called the {\it core} subgraph) so that for some $t>0$: (a) $H$ contains $t$ admissible paths between many pairs of vertices (a robust spreading property); and (b) after contracting or deleting $V(H)$, the remaining graph satisfies suitable minimum degree conditions and yields $k+1-t$ admissible paths between prescribed pairs of vertices. By concatenating these paths appropriately, one obtains the desired $k$ admissible paths in $G$.

A main new tool is that, unlike earlier works where core subgraphs were typically dense structures (complete or complete bipartite, or close to them), this work introduces two new families of core graphs that can be very sparse … while still satisfying properties (a) and (b), called \textit{trigonal graphs} and \textit{tetragonal graphs}, which handle the non-bipartite and bipartite cases, respectively. Because of their sparsity, these core subgraphs can be found in graphs with lower connectivity, providing the crucial ingredient for removing connectivity assumptions.

Organizationally, the proof is reduced later by introducing \textit{$k$-weak} graphs and reducing the proof of the main theorem to this class; specifically, the proof uses the following theorem.

\begin{thm}\label{thm:weak graph}
Let $k\geq 6$ be an integer. If $G$ is a $k$-weak graph not isomorphic to any graph in $\{K_{k+1}, K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal{H}_{k}$, then $G$ contains a cycle of length $r\pmod k$ for every even integer $r$.
\end{thm}

The proof is then split between the subsequent parts, according to whether the host graph is non-bipartite or bipartite.

mcq.question: Let \(k\ge 6\) be an integer and let \(G\) be a graph with minimum degree at least \(k\). Here, a cycle of length \(r \pmod k\) means a cycle whose length is congruent to \(r\) modulo \(k\); \(K_{k+1}\) is the complete graph on \(k+1\) vertices; \(K_{k,k}\) is the complete bipartite graph with both parts of size \(k\); an end-block of \(G\) is a block containing at most one cut-vertex of \(G\), and a non-end-block is any block of \(G\) that is not an end-block; and \(\mathcal H_k\) denotes the exceptional graph family \(\mathcal H_k\). Which description exactly classifies the graphs \(G\) that do not contain a cycle of length \(r \pmod k\) for every even integer \(r\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: Precisely those graphs for which either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(2 \pmod k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(2 \pmod k\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Precisely those graphs for which either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(0 \pmod k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(0 \pmod k\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any such graph must satisfy one of the following: either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: Precisely those graphs for which either \(k\) is odd and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle whose length is congruent to any even residue modulo \(k\); or \(k\) is even and every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to \(K_{k+1}\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle whose length is congruent to any even residue modulo \(k\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: Precisely those graphs for which, whether \(k\) is odd or even, every end-block of \(G\) is isomorphic to a graph in \(\{K_{k+1},K_{k,k}\}\cup \mathcal H_k\), while every non-end-block contains no cycle of length \(2 \pmod k\).

theorem:
\label{thm:intro}
Suppose that $p$ is a prime number, and that $X^{\alpha}$ and $X^{\beta}$ are translation invariant $\mathbf{Z}/\ell$-covers of the Paley graph $X(\bF_p)$ for a prime $\ell \neq p$.  If the adjacency spectra of $X^\alpha$ and $X^\beta$ are equal as multisets, then there is an isomorphism of graphs $X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}$.
sketch:
To prove the stated theorem, the introduction says they (1) first record basic facts about the Paley graphs in \Sthe section on The Paley graphs and their adjacency eigenvalues, and then (2) use character theory to show that the eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix of a covering graph $X^{\alpha}$ are given by the exponential sums
\[
\theta^{\alpha}_{a, k} = \sum_{s \in \bFsquares} \zeta_p^{\tr(as)}\zeta_{\ell}^{k\alpha(s)} \qquad \text{for $a \in \bFq, k \in \mathbf{Z}/\ell$,}
\]
where $\zeta_p=e^{2\pi i/p}$, $\zeta_{\ell}=e^{2\pi i/\ell}$, and $\tr\colon \bF_q^{+}\to\bF_p^{+}$ is the trace homomorphism. It further notes that When $k=0$, the eigenvalues $\theta^{\alpha}_{a,0}$ are the adjacency eigenvalues of the Paley graph $X(\bF_q)$, and can be evaluated explicitly using the quadratic Gauss sum. The introduction concludes that The eigenvalue calculations and the proof of the stated theorem take up \Sthe section on Cyclic covers of Paley graphs and their adjacency eigenvalues.

mcq.question: Fix primes \(p\) and \(\ell\) with \(p \equiv 1 \pmod 4\) and \(\ell \neq p\). Let \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) be the Paley graph on \(\mathbf F_p\) (with vertices \(\mathbf F_p\), and \(x\) adjacent to \(y\) when \(x-y\) is a nonzero square in \(\mathbf F_p\)). Consider connected graph covers of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) with deck group \(\mathbf Z/\ell\) whose defining arc-labeling is invariant under the translation action of the additive group of \(\mathbf F_p\); these are called translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers. Which statement correctly describes the role of the adjacency spectrum in classifying such covers?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets, then \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets, then there is an isomorphism \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) that commutes with the projections to \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) and with the \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-deck actions.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs, then their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For any two connected \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), even without assuming translation invariance of the defining arc-labeling, equality of adjacency spectra as multisets implies \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The same spectral rigidity statement remains valid with \(\mathbf F_p\) replaced by \(\mathbf F_q\) for every finite field \(\mathbf F_q\) with \(q \equiv 1 \pmod 4\): for any prime \(\ell \neq \operatorname{char}(\mathbf F_q)\), equal adjacency spectra of translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers of \(X(\mathbf F_q)\) imply graph isomorphism.

theorem:
\label{T1} Let $(M,\xi,\eta,\Phi,g,\omega)$ be a complete noncompact Sasakian
$(2n+1)$-manifold of nonnegative transverse bisectional curvature. Suppose
that the maximal volume growth holds, then

\begin{enumerate}
\item $M$ is CR-biholomorphic to $\Omega\times\mathbb{R},$ where $\Omega$ is a
pseuodoconvex domain in the $\mathbb{C}^{n}.$

\item In addition for $n=2$, if the transverse bisectional curvature is
positive, then $M$ must be CR-biholomorphic to the standard Heisenberg group
of dimension five
\[\begin{array}
[c]{c}\mathbb{H}_{2}=\mathbb{C}^{2}\times\mathbb{R}.
\end{array}
\]

\end{enumerate},

mcq.question:
Let \((M,\eta,\xi,\Phi,g)\) be a complete noncompact Sasakian \(5\)-manifold. Assume that \(M\) has maximal volume growth, meaning that for some point \(p\in M\),
\[
\lim_{r\to\infty}\frac{\mathrm{Vol}_{\xi}(B_p(r))}{r^{4}}=\alpha_0>0,
\]
where \(B_p(r)\) is the metric ball of radius \(r\) and \(\mathrm{Vol}_{\xi}\) denotes the transverse volume, and assume that the transverse bisectional curvature of the transverse K\"ahler metric \(g^T=g|_{\ker\eta}\) is positive. Up to CR-biholomorphism, which CR manifold must \(M\) be?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The standard Heisenberg group of dimension five, \(\mathbb{H}_2 = \mathbb{C}^2 \times \mathbb{R}\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: A CR manifold CR-biholomorphic to \(\Omega\times \mathbb{R}\), where \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{C}^2\) is a smoothly bounded strictly pseudoconvex domain.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A CR manifold CR-biholomorphic to \(\Omega\times \mathbb{R}\), where \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{C}^2\) is a pseudoconvex domain.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A CR manifold CR-biholomorphic to a nontrivial finite quotient \(\mathbb{H}_2/\Gamma\), where \(\Gamma\) acts freely by CR automorphisms.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The smooth product \(\mathbb{C}^2\times \mathbb{R}\) equipped with the product CR structure induced from the standard complex structure on \(\mathbb{C}^2\).

theorem:
\label{thm:main}
Let $F$ be the complete flag variety of vector subspaces of $\C^4$, isotropic with respect to a nondegenerate skew-symmetric form, endowed with the natural action of the complex torus $H\subset\PSP(4)$ of homothety classes of diagonal matrices. Then the normalized Chow quotient $X$ of $F$ by the action of $H$ is a singular del Pezzo surface of degree $4$ with two singularities of type $A_1$.
sketch: The proof follows the lines of argumentation of prior work, adapted to the particular situation. After preliminaries on torus actions and on the complete flag $F$ of type $\DC_2$, the authors recall some known facts on its fixed points by the action of the maximal torus $H$, and introduce some particular $\C^*$-actions, that later on will play a central role in the study of the boundary divisors of the Chow quotient. Next, they study the combinatorial quotients of $H$-invariant affine charts in $F$, which contain information about the local degeneration of the closure of the general $H$-orbit in $F$. Finally, in the main theorem they prove the main statement of the paper, showing how to construct the Chow quotient $X$ as an inverse limit of combinatorial quotients, and then conclude with a description of the geometry of $X$.

What is the normalized Chow quotient $X$ of $F$ by $H$?

theorem: Given a function/measure which is mean almost periodic with respect to some averaging sequence $\mathcal{A}$, can we find another averaging sequence $\mathcal{B}$ such that the function/measure becomes Besicovitch almost periodic?
sketch:
The approach is as follows: starting with a mean almost periodic measure $\mu$ on $G$, the proof constructs a $G$-invariant probability measure $\mathsf{m}$ on the hull $\mathbb{X}(\mu)$ with pure point spectrum. If the proof establishes that at least one ergodic measure $\omega$ on $\mathbb{X}(\mu)$ has pure point spectrum, one can deduce that $\mathbb{X}(\mu)$ admits Besicovitch almost periodic measures, from which the claim follows.

To obtain such an ergodic $\omega$, the paper proves a more general statement. The proof first uses the following theorem.

\begin{thm}\label{thm:almosteverywhere}
Let $G$ be a second-countable locally compact abelian group acting continuously on a second-countable locally compact space $X$. Suppose $\m \in \ProbG(X)$ and $\rho \in \Prob(\Erg(X))$ satisfy 
\[
\int_X f(x) \, \dd \m(x) = \int_{\Erg(X)} \int_X f(y) \, \dd \omega(y) \, \dd \rho(\omega) \qquad \forall f \in \Cz(X) \,.
\]
If $B \subset \widehat G$ is a Borel set such that $P_\m(B) = \id_{L^2(X,\m)}$, then $P_\omega(B) = \id_{L^2(X,\omega)}$ for $\rho$-a.e. $\omega \in \Erg(X)$. 
\end{thm}

Interpreting the condition $P_\mathsf{m}(B)=\id_{L^2(X,\mathsf{m})}$ as saying that the spectral support of $\mathsf{m}$ is contained in $B$, this theorem yields: if $B\subseteq \widehat{G}$ is Borel and the spectrum of $\mathsf{m}$ is contained in $B$, then for $\rho$-a.s. $\omega\in\operatorname{Erg}(X)$, the spectrum of $\omega$ is also contained in $B$; hence, if $\mathsf{m}$ has pure point spectrum, then so does $\omega$ for $\rho$-a.s. $\omega$.

A key step is extending the Choquet representation
\[
\int_X f\,d\mathsf{m}=\int_{\operatorname{Erg}(X)}\int_X f\,d\omega\,d\rho(\omega)
\]
from $f\in C_0(X)$ to all bounded Borel functions $f\in B(X)$, and then applying it to spectral considerations to transfer spectral support from $L^2(X,\mathsf{m})$ to $L^2(X,\omega)$ for $\rho$-almost all ergodic components.

Finally, they conclude by using this to show that if $\mu$ is mean almost periodic with respect to some van Hove sequence $\mathcal{A}$, then $\mu$ is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to some translated subsequence of $\mathcal{A}$.

mcq.question: Let \(G\) be a second-countable locally compact abelian group. Suppose \(f\in C_u(G)\) is mean almost periodic with respect to a Følner sequence \(\mathcal A\), or \(\mu\in \mathcal M^\infty(G)\) is a translation-bounded measure that is mean almost periodic with respect to a van Hove sequence \(\mathcal A\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of an averaging sequence for which the same function or measure is Besicovitch almost periodic?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists another averaging sequence \(\mathcal{B}\) such that the same function or measure is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal{B}\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists an untranslated subsequence \(\mathcal B=\{A_{k_n}\}\) of \(\mathcal A\) such that the same function or measure is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal B\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists an averaging sequence \(\mathcal B\) such that the same function or measure is mean almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal B\) (for instance, one may take \(\mathcal B=\mathcal A\)).

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a translated subsequence \(\mathcal B=\{t_n+A_{k_n}\}\), determined solely by \(G\) and \(\mathcal A\), such that every function or measure that is mean almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal A\) is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal B\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a single group element \(t\in G\) such that, while keeping the original averaging sequence \(\mathcal A\), the translate \(T_t f\) or the translated measure \(\delta_t*\mu\) is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal A\).

mcq.question:
Let \(n\ge 3\) and \(\Omega=B_R(0)\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) for some \(R>0\), where \(B_r(0)\) denotes the ball of radius \(r\) centered at the origin. Let \(p,q\in\mathbb{R}\) satisfy
\[
p<\frac{n-2}{n-1},\qquad q<\frac{n-2}{n-1},
\]
and define
\[
f(s)=(1+s)^{-p/2},\qquad g(s)=(1+s)^{-q/2}.
\]
Assume the initial data \(u_0,w_0\in W^{1,\infty}(\overline\Omega)\) are positive and radially symmetric. Consider the Neumann problem
\[
\begin{cases}

sketch: The proof strategy can be summarized as follows. The proof first describes a sequence $T_n$ of simplicial $2$-complexes whose fundamental groups have property (T), and so that as $n$ tends to infinity, the girth of links of vertices in $T_n$ also tends to infinity. For large $n$ the proof embeds these complexes $\pi_1$-injectively into negatively curved $3$-pseudomanifolds. To embed such a complex, the proof first deletes a regular neighborhood of the vertices, and thicken the resulting hexagon complex to a $3$-dimensional handlebody. Mirroring the boundary of this handlebody appropriately, the proof obtains a $3$-orbifold with boundary $H_n$. The boundary of $H_n$ contains a $\pi_1$-injective graph corresponding to the union of links of vertices of $T_n$. The $3$-orbifold $H_n$ is (orbifold) covered by a hyperbolic $3$-manifold $M$ with totally geodesic boundary, which is coned off to obtain the $3$-pseudomanifold. The handlebody $H_n$ lifts to $M$, giving rise to a subset of the cone-off homotopy equivalent to $T_n$. They then use quantitative control on various geometric quantities in suitable finite covers together with the results from prior work to construct negatively curved metrics on the cone-offs of such covers.

theorem: Let $\{x_n\}_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ be a sequence in $(U,d)$. If there exists $r\geq 0$ such that $\{x_n\}_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is $r$-$\mathcal{I}$ convergent to $x_*\in U$ then the associated sequence $\{X_n\}_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ of random variables satisfies $X_n\xrightarrow[r]{\mathcal{I}^{\mathbb{P}}} X_*$, where $\mathbb{P}(X_*=x_*)=1$.
sketch:
Assume $\{x_n\}_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ is $r$-$\mathcal{I}$ convergent to $x_*$. Given $\varepsilon>0$, set
\[
A(\varepsilon)=\{n\in\mathbb{N}:d(x_n,x_*)>r+\varepsilon\}\in\mathcal{I}.
\]
Let $A_n=\{\omega\in S: X_n(\omega)=x_n\}$ and $A=\{\omega\in S: X_*(\omega)=x_*\}$, so that $\mathbb{P}(A_n)=1$ and $\mathbb{P}(A)=1$ (and $\mathbb{P}(X_*=x_*)=1$). Since $A(\varepsilon)\in\mathcal{I}$, pick $n\in\mathbb{N}\setminus A(\varepsilon)$; then $d(x_n,x_*)\le r+\varepsilon$, hence
\[
\mathbb{P}(d(X_n,X_*)\le r+\varepsilon)=\mathbb{P}(A_n\cap A)=1.
\]
Therefore, for any $\delta>0$,
\[
\{n\in\mathbb{N}:\mathbb{P}(d(X_n,X_*)>r+\varepsilon)>\delta\}\subseteq A(\varepsilon)\in\mathcal{I},
\]
which yields $X_n\xrightarrow[r]{\mathcal{I}^{\mathbb{P}}} X_*$.,

mcq.question: Fix a probability space \((S,\Delta,\mathbb P)\) and an ideal \(\mathcal I\) on \(\mathbb N\). Let \((U,d)\) be a metric space, let \(\{x_n\}_{n\in\mathbb N}\subset U\), and let \(\{X_n\}_{n\in\mathbb N}\) be the associated constant \(U\)-valued random variables, i.e. \(X_n(s)=x_n\) for all \(s\in S\). Suppose there exist \(r\ge 0\) and \(x_*\in U\) such that \(\{x_n\}_{n\in\mathbb N}\) is \(r\)-\(\mathcal I\) convergent to \(x_*\), meaning that for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \(\{n\in\mathbb N: d(x_n,x_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}\in\mathcal I\). Here \(X_n\xrightarrow[r]{\mathcal I^{\mathbb P}} X_*\) means that for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \(\{n\in\mathbb N: \rho(X_n,X_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}\in\mathcal I\), where \(\rho(X,Y)=\inf\{\eta>0:\mathbb P(d(X,Y)>\eta)\le \eta\}\) is the Ky Fan metric. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a random-variable limit under these assumptions?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and \(X_n\xrightarrow[r]{\mathcal I^{\mathbb P}} X_*\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and, for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \[\{n\in\mathbb N: \rho(X_n,X_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}=\{n\in\mathbb N: d(x_n,x_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}\in\mathcal I.\]

choices.label: C
choices.text: There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and, for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \[\{n\in\mathbb N: \rho(X_n,X_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}\subseteq \{n\in\mathbb N: d(x_n,x_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}.\]

choices.label: D
choices.text: There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and the same conclusion \(X_n\xrightarrow[r]{\mathcal I^{\mathbb P}}X_*\) would still follow if the \(X_n\) were replaced by arbitrary \(U\)-valued random variables satisfying only \(\mathbb P(X_n=x_n)\to 1\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and there is a single set \(A\in\mathcal I\) for which \[\mathbb P(d(X_n,X_*)>r+\varepsilon)=0\quad\text{for every }\varepsilon>0\text{ and every }n\notin A.\]

The proof is by contradiction and, in each one of the blow up scenarios at hand, it is required to find at least one bubble coming with some large enough amount of mass. For this reason they first prove a preliminary minimal mass lemma for solutions of \begin{equation}\label{intro:eq} -\D v_n=\left({\epsilon_n^2+|x|^2}\right)^{\alpha_n}V_n(x)e^{\displaystyle v_n} \qquad\text{in} \,\,\, \Omega, \end{equation} and then prove the $\sup+C\inf$ inequality later.

theorem:
[H\"older regularity]\label{thm:holder}
Let $u$ be a locally bounded weak solution of the first equation in
\begin{equation}\label{1.1}
\begin{cases}\nu_t = \operatorname{div}(\nabla u^m) - 
\operatorname{div}(\n\chi(u,v)u  \nabla v), & \text{in } \mathbb{R}^N \times (t>0), \\[6pt]
v_t = \Delta v - \alpha v + u, & \text{in } \mathbb{R}^N \times (t>0), \\[6pt]
\nu(x,0) = u_0(x) > 0,\quad v(x,0) = v_0(x) \geq 0, & \text{in } \mathbb{R}^N,
\end{cases}
\end{equation}
Then $u$ is locally H\"older continuous in $\mathbb{R}^N \times (0,T]$. 
More precisely, there exist constants $\gamma>0$ and $\alpha \in (0,1)$, depending only on the data, such that for every compact set ${\mathcal{K}} \subset \mathbb{R}^N$,
\[
|u(x_1,t_1) - u(x_2,t_2)| \leq 
\gamma \Big( |x_1-x_2| + \|u\|_\infty^{\tfrac{m-1}{2}} |t_1-t_2|^{1/2} \Big)^{\alpha},
\]
for all $(x_1,t_1), (x_2,t_2) \in {\mathcal K} \times (0,T]$.
sketch: Sections~3--6 are devoted to prove suitable energy estimates in order to adaptat De Giorgi--Di Benedetto method (prior work) to the chemotaxis system. Next, the proof proves logarithmic estimates. Then, the proof proves the main H\"older continuity result.

mcq.question: Let \(p\) be an odd prime, let \(\mathbb F_p\) be the field of \(p\) elements, and let \(c\) be an extra vertex not in \(\mathbb F_p\). A 1-factor of \(K_{p+1}\) is a perfect matching on the vertex set \(\mathbb F_p \cup \{c\}\). For an edge \(\{x,y\}\) with \(x,y\in \mathbb F_p\), define its length to be \(\min\{(x-y) \bmod p,(y-x) \bmod p\}\), where residues modulo \(p\) are taken in \(\{0,1,\dots,p-1\}\); for an edge \(\{x,c\}\), define its length to be \(0\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about 1-factors of \(K_{p+1}\) for every odd prime \(p\) under the condition that every edge joins a quadratic residue to a quadratic non-residue?

theorem:
\label{maintheorem}
		Let $(M, g)$ be a complete noncompact Riemannian manifold whose Ricci curvature is lower bounded, i.e. $\textit{Ric} \geq \lambda g$ for some constant $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$. Moreover, we assume that its injectivity radius has a lower bound, i.e. $\textit{inj} (M,g) \geq i > 0$ for some constant $i > 0$. Then there exists a positive constant $\epsilon = \epsilon (\lambda , i , n) =O(\frac{1}{r_H})$ in the reverse order of the harmonic radius $r_H$ such that for every $\tau > \epsilon$, there exists a constant $C = C(n, \tau , M)$ such that 

		$$
		\sup_{u \in W^{1,n} (M) , ||u||_{1, \tau} \leq 1} \int_M \phi_n (\alpha_n |u|^{\frac{n}{n-1}}) dV_g \leq C. 
		$$ 
		Moreover, $\alpha_n$ is sharp.,
sketch: In the proof of the main theorem one needs to be rigorized about the uniformity of the upper bound of the critical Trudinger--Moser inequality in terms of the bounded domains of the level sets involved. The note give[s] another simple proof by only establishing the uniformity of the bounds of the critical Trudinger--Moser inequality on geodesic balls within the manifolds by modifying the ideas developed by Lam--Lu--Zhang~prior work. This is achieved by using a scaling argument and the asymptotic estimates for the supremum of the subcritical Trudinger--Moser functional established in~prior work, yielding the equivalence between the subcritical and critical Moser--Trudinger inequalties on geodesic balls, but proved here in the opposite direction, i.e. from subcritical inequalities to critical inequalities. As a consequence, one prove[s] the uniform boundedness of the critical Trudinger--Moser inequality on the level set of the functions under consideration in~prior work, thus rigorize[s] the proof by covering the level set by the geodesic balls, so that the same level argument from local inequalities to the global ones used in~prior work are justified.

For \(u\in W^{1,n}(M)\), define \(\|u\|_{1,\tau}:=\|\nabla_g u\|_{L^n(M)}+\tau\|u\|_{L^n(M)}\), let \(\phi_n(t)=e^t-\sum_{k=0}^{n-2} t^k/k!\), let \(\alpha_n=n\omega_{n-1}^{1/(n-1)}\) where \(\omega_{n-1}\) is the surface measure of the unit sphere \(S^{n-1}\subset\mathbb{R}^n\), let \(dV_g\) denote the Riemannian volume element, and let \(r_H\) denote the harmonic radius of \((M,g)\). theorem:
For the complex projective space $\mathbb CP^n$ the generating function of the monomial Chern numbers of the normal bundle $\nu(\mathbb CP^n)$ is
\beq{form}
C^\nu(\mathbb CP^n, t)=(n+1)L_n(t_1,\dots,t_n),
\eeq
where $L_n$ are the Lagrange inversion polynomials.
sketch: The proof derives this theorem from the results of prior work about the Chern-Dold character in complex cobordism theory. From the results of prior work the proof has the following cobordism interpretation of the Lagrange inversion: \[ [\mathbb CP^n]=(n+1)L_n(\tau_1,\dots,\tau_n), \quad \tau_k=\frac{[\Theta^k]}{(k+1)!}, \] where $\Theta^n$ is a smooth theta divisor and $[\Theta^n]$ is its complex cobordism class. Comparing this with prior work, they obtain as a corollary a geometric interpretation in terms of the combinatorics of Stasheff polytopes, and in particular the stated explicit formula for the top Chern number \[ c_n(\nu\,\mathbb CP^n)=(-1)^n(n+1)C_n = (-1)^n {2n \choose n}. \]

theorem:
\label{mainCyc5ecBou}
    Every flow-admissible, cyclically 5-edge-connected, cubic signed graph has a nowhere-zero 6-flow.,
sketch: To prove the main theorem, start with a flow-admissible, cyclically 5-edge-connected cubic signed graph $G$, which has a special nowhere-zero $\Z_6$-flow by a theorem of DeVos et al. Because $G$ is cubic, without loss one may assume every vertex has at most one edge carrying flow into it, and boundary in $\{0,6\}. Preserving these properties, the proof contracts all positive edges. Then a perfect matching in the contracted graph converts the $\Z_6$-flow to a 6-flow, which yields a 6-flow in $G$. The cyclically 5-edge-connected assumption is only used to find the perfect matching.

mcq.question: Let $(G,\sigma)$ be a signed graph, meaning that each edge of the cubic graph $G$ is assigned a sign $\pm 1$. Assume that $G$ is 3-regular, that $(G,\sigma)$ is flow-admissible (that is, it admits a nowhere-zero integer flow in the usual signed-graph sense), and that it is cyclically 5-edge-connected, i.e. every edge cut whose removal separates the graph into two subgraphs each containing a cycle has size at least $5$. Here a nowhere-zero $6$-flow means a nowhere-zero integer flow whose edge values lie in $\{\pm1,\pm2,\pm3,\pm4,\pm5\}$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $(G,\sigma)$?

theorem:
\label{thm1.1} Suppose $\left( f_1^{\prime }\right) \left( f_2\right) \left(
f_3\right) $ hold, then the functional $J$ possesses an unbounded sequence $\left\{ u_k\right\} _{k=1}^\infty $ of critical points satisfying

$\left( i\right) $ $M\left( J,u_k\right) \rightarrow \infty $ as $
k\rightarrow \infty $.

$\left( ii\right) $ $J\left( u_k\right) \rightarrow \infty $ as $
k\rightarrow \infty $.

mcq.question: Assume that the hypotheses \((f_1'), (f_2), (f_3)\) hold for the energy functional \(J\) considered on \(X=H_0^1(\Omega)\). Here, an unbounded sequence means a sequence that is not bounded in \(X\), and for a critical point \(u\), \(M(J,u)\) denotes the generalized Morse index, i.e. the sum of the dimensions of the negative and null eigenspaces of \(J''(u)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the critical points of \(J\) under these assumptions?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: The functional \(J\) has an unbounded sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(M(J,u_k) \to \infty\) and \(J(u_k) \to \infty\) as \(k \to \infty\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The functional \(J\) has an unbounded sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(M(J,u_k)\ge k\) for every \(k\), and the sequence of critical values \(\{J(u_k)\}\) converges to a finite real number.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The functional \(J\) has a sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(M(J,u_k) \to \infty\) as \(k\to\infty\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: For each integer \(k\ge 1\), the functional \(J\) has a critical point \(u_k\) with generalized Morse index exactly \(M(J,u_k)=k\), and \(J(u_k)\to\infty\) as \(k\to\infty\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: The functional \(J\) has an unbounded sequence of critical points \(\{u_k\}_{k=1}^\infty\) such that \(J(u_k)\to\infty\) and the dimension of the null eigenspace of \(J''(u_k)\) is at least \(k\) for every \(k\).

theorem:
\label{thm:main}
If $L$ is a non-split, nontrivial link other than the Hopf link, then
$$
\alpha_3(L)\le 3c(L)-1.
$$
sketch: The proof of the stated theorem is described as constructive. Starting from a reduced link diagram, the authors build a three-page presentation by introducing a suitable binding circle. Then, viewing the diagram as a cellular decomposition of the $2$-sphere, they refine this construction using a contractible subcomplex of the associated cell complex. This refinement allows one to control the number of arcs appearing in the induced three-page presentation and thereby leads to the stated bound, completing the proof of the main theorem.

mcq.question: Let \(L\) be a link in \(S^3\) that is non-split (it cannot be separated into two nonempty parts by a 2-sphere), nontrivial (not an unlink), and not the Hopf link. Let \(c(L)\) denote the crossing number of \(L\), i.e. the minimum number of crossings in any diagram of \(L\). Let \(\alpha_3(L)\) denote the three-page index of \(L\), meaning the minimal number of arcs needed to represent \(L\) in a three-page presentation. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the explicit inequality relating \(\alpha_3(L)\) and \(c(L)\) under these hypotheses?

choices.label: C
choices.text: \[\alpha_3(L)\le 3c(L).\]

choices.label: E
choices.text: \[\alpha_3(L)\le 3c(L)-1\quad\text{for every non-split, nontrivial link }L.\]

sketch:
They take advantage of the Helgason-Fourier analysis on hyperbolic spaces to establish the equivalence between the differential form the referenced equation and the integral form
\[
 u(x)=\int_{\mathbb{H}^n} G_s(x,y)u^p(y)\,dV_y.\tag{the main theorem}
\]
This equivalence plays an essential role in applying the moving plane method to prove the stated theorem. Using the Fourier transform, they derive the precise expression of the Green's function $G_s(\rho)$ and study its asymptotic behaviors near zero and infinity, and these asymptotics are stated as key ingredients for the moving plane method: as $\rho\to0$, $G_s(\rho)\sim \rho^{-n+2s}$ the referenced equation, and as $\rho\to\infty$, $G_s(\rho)\sim \rho^{-1+s}e^{-(n-1)\rho}$ the referenced equation.

mcq.question:
Let \(n\ge 2\) and \(0<s<1\). Let \(\mathbb H^n\) denote the \(n\)-dimensional hyperbolic space, and let \(( -\Delta_{\mathbb H^n})^s\) be the fractional Laplacian on \(\mathbb H^n\). Suppose \(u\in L^q(\mathbb H^n)\) for some \(q>p\) is a nonnegative weak solution of
\[
(-\Delta_{\mathbb H^n})^s u = u^p \quad \text{in } \mathbb H^n,
\]
meaning that for every \(\varphi\in C_c^\infty(\mathbb H^n)\),
\[
\int_{\mathbb H^n} u(x)(-\Delta_{\mathbb H^n})^s\varphi(x)\,dV_x
=\int_{\mathbb H^n} u(x)^p\varphi(x)\,dV_x.
\]
Here, saying that \(u\) is radially symmetric and nondecreasing about a point \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) means that \(u(x)\) depends only on the hyperbolic distance \(d_{\mathbb H^n}(x,x_0)\) and is nondecreasing as that distance increases. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about such solutions in the subcritical and critical cases \(1<p\le \frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), assuming in addition that \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\) when \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\equiv 0\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric and nondecreasing about \(x_0\).

choices.label: B
choices.text: If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\equiv 0\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric and strictly increasing about \(x_0\).

choices.label: C
choices.text: If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\equiv 0\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric about \(x_0\).

choices.label: D
choices.text: If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\equiv 0\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric, nondecreasing about \(x_0\), and strictly positive on \(\mathbb H^n\).

choices.label: E
choices.text: If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\) is strictly positive and radially symmetric about some \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric and nondecreasing about \(x_0\).

theorem:
\label{thm-KF}
Suppose that $K$ is a number field and $e$ a positive integer. For almost all $\sigma \in G_K^e$, any finite extension of $\overline{K}(\sigma)$ is both torally Kummer-faithful and AVKF, and thus, Kummer-faithful.,
sketch: The proof of the main theorem is split into two parts: the \emph{torally Kummer-faithfulness} part and the \emph{AVKF-ness} part, proved next and then later, respectively. The approaches are described as similar. First, the problem is reformulated by reducing it to a \emph{calculation of measures} (for the AVKF-ness part, this reduction was done by Jarden and Petersen~prior work; for the torally Kummer-faithfulness part, it uses a refinement of the proof of the weaker version~prior work). Then, the required measure calculations are carried out. Finally, the proof is concluded via a \emph{combinatorial approach} similar to Zywina~prior work (see also~prior work).

mcq.question: Let \(K\) be a number field, let \(e\ge 1\) be an integer, and write \(G_K=\mathrm{Gal}(\overline K/K)\), where \(\overline K\) is a fixed algebraic closure of \(K\). For \(\sigma=(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_e)\in G_K^e\), let \(\overline K(\sigma)\) denote the fixed field of the subgroup of \(G_K\) generated by \(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_e\). Here “almost all \(\sigma\in G_K^e\)” means all \(\sigma\) outside a subset of Haar measure zero in \(G_K^e\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about every finite extension \(L\) of \(\overline K(\sigma)\) for almost all \(\sigma\in G_K^e\)?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: For almost all \(\sigma\in G_K^e\), every finite extension \(L/\overline K(\sigma)\) is both torally Kummer-faithful and AVKF, and hence is Kummer-faithful.

choices.label: B
choices.text: For almost all \(\sigma\in G_K^e\), every finite extension \(L/\overline K(\sigma)\) is Kummer-faithful, and this already forces \(L\) to be both torally Kummer-faithful and AVKF.

choices.label: C
choices.text: For almost all \(\sigma\in G_K^e\), every finite extension \(L/\overline K(\sigma)\) is Kummer-faithful.

choices.label: D
choices.text: For almost all \(\sigma\in G_K^e\), every algebraic extension \(L/\overline K(\sigma)\) is both torally Kummer-faithful and AVKF, and hence is Kummer-faithful.

choices.label: E
choices.text: For almost all \(\sigma\in G_K^e\), every finite extension \(L/\overline K(\sigma)\) is torally Kummer-faithful, while \(\overline K(\sigma)\) itself is AVKF; hence every such \(L\) is Kummer-faithful.

mcq.question:
Let \(\Omega=G\times(-h,0)\) for some horizontal domain \(G\subset\mathbb R^2\), with horizontal variables \((x,y)\), vertical variable \(z\), and boundary parts \(\Gamma_u=G\times\{0\}\), \(\Gamma_b=G\times\{-h\}\), and \(\Gamma_l=\partial G\times(-h,0)\). Let \(u=(v,w)\) solve the three-dimensional primitive equations
\[
\partial_t v+u\cdot \nabla v-\Delta v+\nabla_H \pi=0,\qquad \partial_z\pi=0,\qquad \operatorname{div}u=0
\quad\text{in }\Omega\times(0,T),
\]
with initial condition
\[
v|_{t=0}=a \quad\text{in }\Omega.
\]
Here \(\nabla_H=(\partial_x,\partial_y)\), \(\operatorname{div}_H\) is the horizontal divergence, and the vertical average is
\[
\bar a(x,y):=\frac1h\int_{-h}^0 a(x,y,z)\,dz.
\]
Assume the initial data satisfy \(a\in H^1(\Omega)\), \(a_z:=\partial_z a\in L^3(\Omega)\), the compatibility condition \(\operatorname{div}_H\bar a=0\), and horizontal periodicity on \(\Gamma_l\). In the mixed boundary-condition case, also assume \(a=0\) on \(\Gamma_b\). Suppose moreover that \(u,\pi\) are periodic on \(\Gamma_l\times(0,T)\) and that either
\[
\partial_z v=0,\quad w=0 \quad\text{on }(\Gamma_u\cup\Gamma_b)\times(0,T),
\]
or
\[
\partial_z v=0,\quad w=0 \quad\text{on }\Gamma_u\times(0,T),
\qquad
v=0,\quad w=0 \quad\text{on }\Gamma_b\times(0,T).
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(v\) for every \(t\in[0,T]\) under these assumptions?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text:
It satisfies the uniform estimate
\[
\|v(t)\|_{H^1(\Omega)}^2+\int_0^t \|\nabla^2 v(s)\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2\,ds
\le B(a)e^{C\|a\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2}
\quad\forall t\in[0,T],
\]
where \(B(a)\) is a polynomial function of \(\|a\|_{H^1(\Omega)}\) and \(\|a_z\|_{L^3(\Omega)}\), and \(C>0\) depends only on \(\Omega\); however, this conclusion holds only in the fully Neumann case, while in the mixed boundary-condition case one only gets a time-dependent bound.

choices.label: C
choices.text:
It satisfies the bound
\[
\|v(t)\|_{H^1(\Omega)}^2
\le B(a)e^{C\|a\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2}
\quad\forall t\in[0,T],
\]
where \(B(a)\) is a polynomial function of \(\|a\|_{H^1(\Omega)}\) and \(\|a_z\|_{L^3(\Omega)}\), and \(C>0\) depends only on \(\Omega\).

choices.label: D
choices.text:
It satisfies the a priori estimate
\[
\|v(t)\|_{H^1(\Omega)}^2+\int_0^t \|\nabla^2 v(s)\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2\,ds
\le B(a)e^{C\|a\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2}
\quad\forall t\in[0,T],
\]
but only after strengthening the hypothesis to \(a_z\in L^4(\Omega)\); under merely \(a_z\in L^3(\Omega)\), one has only the time-dependent proposition-type estimate.

choices.label: E
choices.text:
Under the stated assumptions one can conclude only the estimate
\[
\|v(t)\|_{H^1(\Omega)}
\le B(a,t)e^{C(\sqrt t+1)\|a\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2}
\quad\forall t\in[0,T],
\]
where \(B(a,t)\) is a polynomial function of \(\|a\|_{H^1(\Omega)}\), \(\|a\|_{L^8(\Omega)}\), \(\|a_z\|_{L^4(\Omega)}\), and \(\sqrt t\); in particular, no time-independent bound of the form stated in A is available from these assumptions.

theorem:
[Monotonicity]\label{monotonicity}
    For all $f \in \Rb\wspc{T}$, the function $x \mapsto f \circ x$ for $x \in \mathbb{U}^{>\mathbb{R}}$ is strictly increasing if $f' > 0$, strictly decreasing if $f' < 0$, and constant if $f' = 0$.

mcq.question: Let \(T>1\) be a fixed transseries, and let \(\mathbb{R}\wspc{T}\) denote the corresponding field of omega-series in \(T\). Let \(\mathbb{U}^{>\mathbb{R}}\) be the ordered set of inputs \(x\) for which the composition \(f\circ x\) is defined. For a given \(f\in \mathbb{R}\wspc{T}\), consider the map \(x\mapsto f\circ x\) on \(\mathbb{U}^{>\mathbb{R}}\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the monotonicity of this map when \(f'\) is everywhere positive, everywhere negative, or identically zero?

correct_choice.label: A
correct_choice.text: One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sign of \(f'\) gives the stated monotonicity conclusion for omega-series obtained from \(T\) by finitely many applications of \(\exp\) and \(\log\), but for genuinely infinite omega-series one can in general conclude only non-strict monotonicity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If \(f'>0\), then the map \(x\mapsto f\circ x\) is increasing; if \(f'<0\), then it is decreasing; and if \(f'=0\), then it is constant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The map \(x\mapsto f\circ x\) is strictly increasing if \(f'>0\), strictly decreasing if \(f'<0\), and only locally constant on \(\mathbb{U}^{>\mathbb{R}}\) if \(f'=0\), with no global constancy conclusion in general.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The sign of \(f'\) determines the monotonicity of \(x\mapsto f\circ x\) only for those \(f\) that satisfy an additional weak mean-value estimate; for arbitrary omega-series, the derivative sign alone does not suffice.

theorem:
[Boundedness of augmented irregularity]\label{thm:1-3}  Let $(X, D)$ be a $\mathcal C$-pair that satisfies one of the following conditions.
  \begin{enumerate}
  \item\label{il:1-3-1} The analytic variety $X$ is compact Kähler and $(X,D)$
    is locally uniformizable.

  \item\label{il:1-3-2} The analytic variety $X$ is projective and $(X,D)$ is
    divisorially log terminal (=dlt).
  \end{enumerate}
  If $(X,D)$ is special, then $q⁺(X, D) ≤ \dim X$.
sketch:
For Campana’s classic result, it suffices to show that the Albanese map of a special manifold is necessarily surjective; arguing by contraposition, Campana studies the case when the Albanese map is not surjective and, using positivity results for sheaves of differentials on varieties of maximal Albanese dimension, constructs a sheaf of differentials where equality in the Bogomolov-Sommese inequality is attained, which show[s] that the underlying space cannot be special.

In the present setting, adapted differentials take the role that ordinary differentials play, but there is in general no ‘adapted Albanese map’, and even when an Albanese exists, the classical equality between the irregularity and the dimension of the Albanese variety is not true in general. The authors overcome this problem by considering the classic Albanese of suitable covers, where adapted differentials define a foliation. Although the foliation typically has Zariski dense leaves, they leverage ideas from Catanese’s work on generalized Castelnuovo–De Franchis theorems and ‘strict wedge subspaces of differentials’ to obtain positivity results for the foliated variety that can be used in lieu of the classic arguments.

A technical input is that the proof of the stated theorem relies in part on an extension theorem: adapted reflexive 1-forms on $(X,D)$ extend to log resolutions of singularities (the referenced theorem). For 1-forms this approach work[s] particularly well, yielding Corollary the referenced result, which bounds the \(\mathcal C\)-Kodaira–Iitaka dimension of rank-one subsheaves of adapted one-forms on covers of special pairs.

mcq.question: Let $(X,D)$ be a $\mathcal C$-pair. sketch:
The post-theorem discussion indicates that the proof of the main theorem is obtained by transferring pure homological dimension across functors that preserve the pure structure. Concretely:
\begin{itemize}
\item One interprets purity in a compactly generated triangulated category $\mathsf{T}$ via the restricted Yoneda embedding $\msf{y}$: it sends triangles to long exact sequences, and a triangle goes to a short exact sequence precisely when the connecting morphism is a phantom map; these are the \emph{pure triangles}. Thus purity can be thought of as the study of phantom maps.
\item The embedding $\msf{y}$ establishes an equivalence between the pure structure on $\mathsf{T}$ and the pure structure on $\Flat{\mathsf{T}^{\mrm{c}}}$, where there are enough pure injectives/projectives. One therefore defines pure projective/injective dimensions and the pure global dimension on $\Flat{\mathsf{T}^{\mrm{c}}}$, and the pure global dimension of $\mathsf{T}$ is defined to be the pure global dimension of $\Flat{\mathsf{T}^{\mrm{c}}}$.
\item The main tool used to prove the main theorem is to analyze how pure homological dimension transfers along \emph{definable} (interpretation) functors, i.e. functors preserving coproducts, products, and pure triangles. Any such $F\colon\mathsf{T}\to\mathsf{U}$ induces a functor $\widehat{F}\colon\Flat{\mathsf{T}^{\mrm{c}}}\to\Flat{\mathsf{U}^{\mrm{c}}}$ compatible with $\msf{y}$, so it suffices to work in this accessible setting.
\item The transfer is implemented via the following theorem.

\begin{thmx}\label{intro:thmb}(the referenced proposition)
Let $F\colon\A\to\B$ be a definable functor between finitely accessible categories with products, and suppose that $F$ admits a left adjoint $\Lambda\colon\B\to\A$. Then there are isomorphisms
\[
\mrm{PExt}_{\msf{B}}^{n}(B,F(A))\simeq \mrm{PExt}_{\A}^{n}(\Lambda(B),A)
\]
for all $A\in\A, B\in\B$ and $n\geq 0$.
\end{thmx}

In particular, for a definable $F\colon\mathsf{A}\to\mathsf{B}$ with left adjoint $\Lambda$, there are isomorphisms
\[\mathrm{PExt}_{\mathsf{B}}^{n}(B,F(A))\simeq \mathrm{PExt}_{\mathsf{A}}^{n}(\Lambda(B),A)\]
for all $n\ge 0$, where $\mathrm{PExt}$ is computed using pure projective/pure injective resolutions and measure[s] the pure homological dimension of objects.
\item In relation to the main theorem, it is through the above isomorphisms applied to particular choices of definable functors between $\msf{Mod}(R)$, $\mathsf{D}(R)$, and $\mathsf{D}(S)$ that one obtains the result.
\end{itemize}
This is the only proof-outline content given for the main theorem in the provided post-theorem text.
